# You are ready to teach {#sec-summary .unnumbered}

If you have worked through this handbook, you now have everything you need to run **Git for Science**.

You understand why the workshop is shaped the way it is: taught on a need-to-know basis, in a room that is safe to fail in, with collaboration at the center (@sec-philosophy). You know the journey runs from pre-course work through four hours of hands-on practice to a forward-looking wrap-up, and that learners pick up R, Quarto, and the terminal as side benefits along the way (@sec-course-shape).

You have the setup in place: the accounts and software every learner and the instructor needs (Part 2), the three GitHub organizations and their repositories (@sec-create-orgs), and a published cohort website (@sec-website). You know how to run the day, from pre-flight checks (@sec-before) to the timed run of show (@sec-run-of-show).

And you know how to keep it going: spinning up the next cohort from the templates (@sec-new-iteration), and keeping the material a genuine Open Educational Resource through its license and citation (@sec-citation-license).

## What is still to come

Two things in this handbook are deliberately unfinished, and they are yours to develop:

- **The live drawings** (@sec-live-drawings) are placeholders. Develop them and replace the placeholders as you go; @sec-appendix-drawings tracks the list. TODO: The central drawings will be done by me, but there should be a place where drawings for each iteration are captured. Also those of learners. How can that best be achieved? Maybe by adding photos as issues. This guidebook requires a smart way to cover each iterations specifics and learnings.

- **The follow-up workshop** on agentic coding for collaboration and scientific work builds on these foundations. It is a separate effort, out of scope here, but this workshop is its prerequisite.
